sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Jerry, a 45-year-old male, presented with an ankle injury sustained during a basketball game with his son.
He reported landing awkwardly after being pushed by his son and twisting his ankle, resulting in pain, swelling, and instability on the outside of the ankle.
He denied any previous ankle injuries or chronic conditions.
He has been experiencing difficulty walking and feels the ankle is not stable.
He recently started working out and does not want to disrupt his routine.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The examination revealed exquisite tenderness laterally, tenderness over the medial deltoid region, swelling on the lateral side of the ankle, intact skin, brisk capillary refill, and no malalignment.
Test Results:
X-ray: A displaced lateral malleolus fracture at the Weber C level with no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a displaced lateral malleolar fracture with ankle instability.
Problem list: Lateral malleolar fracture; ankle instability; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam for pain and inflammation.
I recommended icing the ankle and elevating it regularly, especially since the patient works from home.
I provided crutches to assist with mobility.
I advised the patient to continue coaching his son's basketball team in April as planned, but to avoid putting weight on the ankle for the next three months.
We will reassess the patient's progress in three months.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in three months for follow-up.
